title: Granola
subtitle: Jumbo oats baked with honey and oil until golden and clustered.
excerpt: Jumbo oats mixed with honey, olive oil, and seeds, then baked slowly at a low temperature until golden and clumped. Keeps for three weeks in a jar. Better than any shop-bought version.
sourceNotes: Granola was invented by Dr John Harvey Kellogg in the late nineteenth century as a health food for patients at his Battle Creek Sanitarium in Michigan. The modern sweetened, baked version with nuts and dried fruit became commercially prominent in the 1960s American health food movement. It reached British supermarkets in the 1970s and has been a standard breakfast staple since, though homemade versions became widespread as health-food culture grew in the 2000s.

content.text: The two secrets to good granola are low temperature and not disturbing it while it bakes. Low heat means the oats toast rather than burn, and the honey caramelises slowly to produce clusters. If you stir it mid-bake, the clusters break up.
The recipe below is the base. Once you know the technique, adjust the nuts, seeds, and spices to taste. Dried fruit goes in after baking. It turns leathery if baked with the oats.
A large baking tray lined with baking paper and a large mixing bowl.

Heat the oven to 150 °C fan. Put the oats, almonds, and pumpkin seeds in a large bowl. Mix {{honey}} of honey, {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract, {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt together in a small bowl or jug. Pour over the oats and mix well until everything is coated.

Spread the mixture in an even layer across a lined large baking tray. Press it down gently with the back of a spoon, this encourages clusters. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until golden. Do not stir during baking. The granola will still feel soft when it comes out of the oven; it firms up as it cools.
Leave the granola to cool completely on the tray, about 15 minutes. Once cool, break into clusters and mix in the dried cranberries. Transfer to an airtight jar. Keeps for three weeks.

items.fix: Press down firmly before baking and do not touch until cooled. Add an extra tablespoon of honey next time.
items.cause: Either stirred during baking, or not enough honey to bind.
items.symptom: Granola has no clusters, just loose oats

items.fix: 150 °C fan is deliberately low. Check your oven temperature with a thermometer, ovens often run hotter than they show. Check at 25 minutes.
items.cause: Oven temperature too high, or baked for too long.
items.symptom: Granola is burnt on the edges

items.fix: Return to the oven for another 5 to 8 minutes. The granola should be a deep golden colour before removing.
items.symptom: Granola is pale and soft after cooling

items.fix: Always stir dried fruit in after the granola has cooled. It cannot be rescued once baked in.
items.cause: Added before baking rather than after.
items.symptom: Dried fruit is tough and leathery

content.text: Granola sits in the American tradition of breakfast cereal as health food. That line runs from Kellogg and Post in the nineteenth century through the granola bars and trail mix of the 1970s backpacking scene. It came to British kitchens first through health food shops, then through supermarket ranges. The homemade version spread from the mid-2000s through food blogs and brunch culture. It is low effort, keeps for weeks, and beats the shop-bought kind on taste. That mix made it one of the most common batch-cook recipes in British home kitchens.
